[By Sam Tanenhaus](http://marginalrevolution.com?action=user_content_redirect&uuid=8c15d5e86a057714fb145f763bd295d2cf8a75daa07e0a867bd1a51b66d09750&blog_id=42693868&post_id=91090&user_id=262258391&subs_id=225460721&signature=2d4b5ebc0cb14ddffad81e2e152cdcc1&email_name=new-post&user_email=ishan.mail@gmail.com&encoded_&email_id=556288d64f5d804392fb90b4644b83bb). I held off reading this book at first, as I felt I already knew a lot about Buckley and his life. But it is excellent. Very well written and engaging throughout. I learned a good deal, and it is one of the best books on the history of the American 20th century right wing.
As a youth, watching Firing Line, I frequently was frustrated that Buckley was not more analytical, and that he sometimes spoke in such a roundabout manner. In part I wanted to expand Conversations with Tyler to fill that gap. I am also indebted to Buckley for first getting me interested in Bach. So he played a very definite role in my life.
